A/B Test Report: Mobile Payment Screen Redesign

Generated: 2026-05-19  |  Experiment start: July 23, 2021  |  Platform: Mobile only

1. Executive Summary

Recommendation: DO NOT LAUNCH

The new payment screen UI produced no statistically significant improvement on any primary business metric. Conversion rate in the test group was 2.87% vs 3.06% in control (difference: -0.00 pp, p=0.736 — not significant, and the direction is negative). Average Revenue Per Payer was $644 (test) vs $491 (control), also not significant (p=0.131). There is no statistical evidence that launching would increase revenue or conversions.

2. What Was Tested and Why

The product team redesigned the in-app payment screen UI for mobile users to increase successful purchases of in-game currency. Only the screen layout was changed — prices, currency amounts, and game mechanics were unchanged. The experiment ran from July 23, 2021, splitting new registrations into: a control group (original screen) and a test group (redesigned screen).

This game operates on a whale monetization model — a small fraction of high-spending users drives the majority of revenue. The analysis therefore covers overall conversion rate, average payment amount, total revenue per user, and the impact on the whale segment separately.

3. Who Was Included

  • Platform: Mobile users only (the redesign was built for mobile). Non-mobile users were excluded.
  • Registration date: Only users registered on or after July 23, 2021 (experiment start). Pre-existing users were excluded to avoid contamination.
  • Successful payments only (failed transactions excluded from revenue metrics).
  • Final cohort: Control: 6,070 users  |  Test: 5,961 users

Conversion Rate

Avg Revenue Per Payer (ARPP)

5. Statistical Significance — Plain English

Statistical tests used:

  • Conversion Rate: Two-proportion Z-test (one-tailed). Tests whether the test group has a higher conversion rate than control. P-value: 0.736.
  • Avg Revenue Per Payer: Mann-Whitney U test (non-parametric; appropriate because payment amounts are heavily right-skewed due to whale spending). P-value: 0.131.
  • Revenue Per User: Mann-Whitney U test. P-value: 0.730.
All three primary metrics: p-value > 0.05 — no statistically significant effect found. In plain terms: the observed differences between test and control are consistent with random variation. We cannot conclude that the new design changes user behavior.

Confidence interval for conversion rate difference: [-0.008 pp, +0.004 pp] (95% CI). The interval includes zero and the lower bound is negative — the redesign may actually reduce conversion rate.

The experiment was adequately powered for conversion rate (required ~2,115 users/group for 1 pp MDE; actual: ~6,070; estimated power: 90%).

6. Whale Impact Analysis

Whale users are the top spenders whose combined spend accounts for 75% of total revenue. The whale threshold for this experiment is $1,001 in post-experiment revenue.

MetricControlTestDifference
Number of Whales 22 23 +1
Whale Share of Payers 11.8% 13.5% +1.6 pp
Avg Revenue per Whale User $2,974 $3,736 +25.6%
Whale Revenue Per User (all users) $10.78 $14.41 +33.7%
Whale metrics trend positive — test group shows higher whale share and higher average whale spend — but the sample is too small (22–23 whales per group) to draw reliable conclusions. Mann-Whitney U test on whale payment amounts: p=0.486 — not significant. This is a directional positive signal and warrants further investigation, but it is not sufficient justification for a full launch.

8. Sanity Checks

Overall PASS: Gender, age group, country group, and traffic source distributions are balanced between test and control (chi-square p > 0.05). Randomization appears valid for these dimensions.
FLAG: Device operating system (system) distribution differs between groups (chi-square p = 0.004) in the mobile cohort. This imbalance is small in practical terms but represents a methodological caveat.

9. Risks and Caveats

  • Revenue metric power: Due to extreme variance in payment amounts (Gini ≈ 0.99; a handful of whale users dominate revenue), the experiment would require hundreds of thousands of users to reliably detect a 10% change in RPU. Revenue-based conclusions are therefore underpowered.
  • OS imbalance: A statistically significant difference in device OS mix was found between groups, which may introduce minor confounding.
  • Whale sample size: Only 22–23 whale users per group — not enough for reliable statistical inference on whale-level effects.
  • One-tailed tests: All tests were designed to detect improvement in test. They are less sensitive to detecting harm (negative effects on control).
  • Novelty effect: Users may behave differently when encountering a new UI for the first time. A longer experiment would reduce this risk.
  • Scope: This analysis covers mobile users only. Impact on other platforms is unknown.

10. Final Recommendation

DO NOT LAUNCH

The new payment screen UI does not demonstrate a statistically significant improvement over the current design on any primary metric. Conversion rate was marginally lower in the test group (2.87% vs 3.06%, p=0.736). Revenue metrics showed no significant change. While whale spending trended upward in the test group, the whale sample is too small to draw firm conclusions.

Suggested next steps:

  • Conduct qualitative UX research to understand why the redesign did not lift conversion.
  • Consider a targeted redesign experiment focused specifically on the whale segment, potentially with a longer run time and a larger cohort.
  • Investigate the device OS imbalance before running the next experiment.
  • For future experiments testing revenue metrics, plan for a much larger sample size (the whale model creates extremely high variance that requires large cohorts).
Report generated automatically from A/B test data  |  Mobile users registered on/after July 23, 2021  |  Statistical significance threshold: α = 0.05
